explicit_text: I was born in Los Angeles, California, on March 18, 1933. Growing up there, it was the starting point of my life and family story. I also have relatives like my Aunt Suzy Johnson who still lives in Los Angeles. It’s where I returned to after summers spent in Bakersfield and where I met my wife, Carmen Garrott.

explicit_text: California has been central to my life story, from my birth in Los Angeles to summers spent in Bakersfield. My mother's side of the family lived in Bakersfield, which is about 115 miles north of Los Angeles, up Highway 99. Growing up in California meant experiencing both city life and the agricultural fields where I worked as a teenager.

explicit_text: My father is John Thomas Riddle, Sr., one of twelve children and right in the middle of the siblings. He was part of a large and diverse family, with a preacher father and relatives involved in everything from communism to numbers racketeering. He and my mother raised me in Los Angeles, and I have many memories connected to his side of the family.

He and my mother raised me in a Southern California city, and I have many memories connected to his side of the family.

explicit_text: My mother is Helen Louise Wheeler, born in Bloomington, Indiana, but she lived much of her life in California. We spent many summers visiting her family in Bakersfield, which was a big part of my childhood. Her mother, Emma, was an incredible cook and caterer, and my Uncle George, her son, was a World War II veteran with a complicated life.

explicit_text: Bakersfield, California, is where my mother’s family lived and where we spent many summers during my childhood. It’s about 115 miles north of Los Angeles, along what used to be Highway 99. Those summers were filled with family gatherings, working in the fields, and memories of my grandmother Emma’s cooking and chicken coop.

explicit_text: World War II affected my family through my Uncle George Wheeler, who served in the war. After returning, he struggled with alcoholism and became a complex figure in our family. His experiences and struggles were part of the stories I heard growing up.

explicit_text: Emma was my grandmother on my mother’s side, and she was an amazing cook who even catered for restaurants and hotels from her kitchen. I have vivid memories of her chicken coop and the summers spent at her house in Bakersfield. She was a central figure in our family gatherings and childhood experiences.

explicit_text: George Wheeler was my uncle, my mother’s brother, who served in World War II and later struggled with alcoholism. He was a kind of philosopher figure in the family, sharing wisdom despite his personal difficulties. I spent many summer nights listening to him under the grape arbor in Bakersfield.

explicit_text: Drisdom was my grandfather on my mother’s side, who worked on the railroad. I remember him having a drawer full of pocket watches with lids that popped open, which fascinated me as a child. He was part of the complex family history involving multiple marriages and names.

explicit_text: I met Carmen Garrott after returning to Los Angeles from Bakersfield when I was about eighteen or nineteen. We married and have been together for thirty-nine years now. She is a significant part of my life story and family.

explicit_text: C. Morton Riddle was my paternal grandfather, a tall, thin preacher with white hair who stood very straight. He was a strong figure in my father’s side of the family, which included many siblings and diverse personalities. His presence was a defining part of our family heritage.
